title: Brother Can You Spare Two Dimes

jokes.text: D'oh!
jokes.setup: Homer realizes something has gone wrong
jokes.punchline: His signature frustrated exclamation
jokes.explanation: Iconic catchphrase that defines Homer's character - perfect economy and character integration

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Hey, Homer, can I borrow your underwear? / Nah.
jokes.setup: Coworker needs underwear for physical exam
jokes.punchline: Homer's casual refusal to share underwear
jokes.explanation: Simple workplace absurdity with Homer's typical bluntness

dialogue.speaker: Coworker
dialogue.line: Hey Homer, I need to borrow some underwear for my physical exam.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No.

jokes.text: This man has 104% body fat.
jokes.punchline: Physically impossible body fat percentage
jokes.explanation: Mathematically impossible result perfectly captures Homer's extreme unhealthiness

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: Well, Homer, according to these tests, you have a body fat percentage of 270%.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo! I'm in the top 1%!

jokes.text: Hey, no eating in the tank. / Go to hell.
jokes.setup: Homer is eating during medical examination
jokes.punchline: His hostile response to being told to stop eating
jokes.explanation: Homer's defiant response to reasonable medical instruction shows his character

dialogue.speaker: Doctor
dialogue.line: You need to stop eating.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Hey, I'm trying to enjoy my last meal before you tell me I'm dying!

jokes.text: Oh, the usual-- stand in front of this. Open that. Pull down this. Bend over. Spread apart that. Turn your head that way. Cough.
jokes.setup: Marge asks about his day at work
jokes.punchline: Homer describes intimate medical exam procedures as casual work routine
jokes.explanation: Homer's obliviousness to the inappropriateness of sharing medical exam details

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: So how was your day at work?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Oh, you know, the usual. Had a prostate exam, colonoscopy, some guy stuck his finger in places I didn't know I had places. Just another Tuesday at the plant.

jokes.text: A cabbagehead from sector 7-G.
jokes.setup: Burns asks who Homer Simpson is
jokes.punchline: Burns dismissively describes Homer as a 'cabbagehead'
jokes.explanation: Burns's creative insult combined with workplace specificity

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Who is Homer Simpson?

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Some cabbagehead, I suppose.

jokes.text: Let me make one thing clear. I want your legal advice. I even pay for it. But to me, you're all vipers. You live on personal injury, on divorces. You live on pain and misery! But I'm rambling. Anybody want coffee?
jokes.setup: Burns building up angry rant about lawyers
jokes.punchline: Suddenly shifts to polite coffee offer mid-rant
jokes.explanation: Burns's aristocratic politeness interrupting his own venomous tirade is perfectly in character

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Those blasted lawyers! Always scheming, always demanding more money! They're parasites, bloodsuckers, the scourge of decent society! I'll have them all—

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Would you care for some coffee?

jokes.text: Want it black, don't you? Black like your heart.
jokes.punchline: Burns connects coffee preference to moral character
jokes.explanation: Perfect Burns line - mixing mundane hospitality with cruel personal attack

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Coffee?

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: No thank you.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: You know, I like a man who doesn't drink coffee. Shows he has nothing to hide. A man with a clear conscience doesn't need stimulants.

jokes.text: I hate you all so much! I'm sorry. It's my problem. I'll deal with it.
jokes.setup: Burns expressing hatred for his lawyers
jokes.punchline: Immediately apologizes and takes responsibility
jokes.explanation: Burns's sudden self-awareness and politeness after venting hatred

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: I hate my lawyers. They're incompetent fools who couldn't argue their way out of a paper bag.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: Actually, I apologize. That was unfair to the lawyers. I take full responsibility for my anger.

jokes.text: Oh, a cash settlement. I could have figured that out, you buttoned-down maggot!
jokes.punchline: Burns insults lawyer while acknowledging obvious advice
jokes.explanation: Burns's creative insult 'buttoned-down maggot' while admitting the advice was obvious

dialogue.speaker: Lawyer
dialogue.line: I suggest a cash settlement, sir.

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: You're a crook and an idiot. But you're right.

jokes.text: Yeah, I used to be rich. I owned Mickey Mouse massage parlors. Then those Disney sleazeballs shut me down. I said, 'I'll change the logo-- put Mickey's pants on.' Some guys you just can't reason with.
jokes.setup: Herb explaining his past business ventures
jokes.punchline: Absurd Mickey Mouse massage parlor business and Disney's 'unreasonable' response
jokes.explanation: Absurd business concept with Herb's obliviousness to obvious copyright issues

dialogue.speaker: Herb Powell
dialogue.line: So I opened up this Mickey Mouse massage parlor, right? Best idea I ever had!

dialogue.speaker: Herb Powell
dialogue.line: Disney sued me. Said I was infringing on their intellectual property. Can you believe that? Mickey Mouse massage parlor!

dialogue.speaker: Herb Powell
dialogue.line: I said, 'It's just a massage!' They said it was trademark infringement. I said, 'What's the big deal? It's therapeutic!'

dialogue.speaker: Herb Powell
dialogue.line: Turns out Disney's real unreasonable about their rodent.

jokes.text: You ever drive a Tempura hatchback?
jokes.setup: Herb explaining his car company strategy of Japanese names
jokes.punchline: Car named after Japanese food instead of proper Japanese name
jokes.explanation: Clever wordplay showing Herb's misunderstanding of 'Japanese names' for cars

dialogue.speaker: Herb Powell
dialogue.line: I'll name the car after Japanese food instead of a proper Japanese name.

jokes.text: I'm ruined!
jokes.punchline: Immediate realization of financial disaster
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback to earlier episode disaster with immediate dramatic reaction

dialogue.speaker: Herb Powell
dialogue.line: I spared no expense! This car will revolutionize the automotive industry!

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: So what do you think, Mr. Powell? Pretty great, huh?

dialogue.speaker: Herb Powell
dialogue.line: Homer, I've sunk my entire fortune into this vehicle. My life savings, my house, everything!

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: That's... that's wonderful, sir.

dialogue.speaker: Herb Powell
dialogue.line: Wait... what have I done?

jokes.text: Overblown, don't you think? What about New Coke? Hey! I invented that.
jokes.punchline: Herb claiming responsibility for another famous business disaster
jokes.explanation: Escalates Herb's history of business disasters by connecting him to real-world failure

dialogue.speaker: Herb Powell
dialogue.line: Forbes called my business plan overblown? Well, I'm responsible for the Edsel, the Betamax, and New Coke!

jokes.text: Well, I'm licked. / Me too.
jokes.punchline: Two characters immediately give up after hearing it
jokes.explanation: Simple deflation of motivational rhetoric with immediate surrender

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, in America, any man can pull himself up by his bootstraps and achieve his dreams!

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: Yeah, right. I'm going back to the bar.

dialogue.speaker: Lenny
dialogue.line: This is too much work. I'm taking a nap.

jokes.text: I do this for Stainmaster carpets... proud sponsor of the living room Olympics.
jokes.setup: Homer about to vault over couch
jokes.punchline: Treating living room activity as sponsored Olympic event
jokes.explanation: Homer's grandiose treatment of mundane couch-vaulting with fake sponsorship

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And the crowd goes wild as Homer Simpson approaches the couch for his signature move. He's training hard, folks. This is his moment.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: This event is brought to you by Duff Beer and donuts. The official sponsors of extreme couch vaulting.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And here he goes... *grunts* ...three, two, one... *crashes into couch* ...AND HE STICKS THE LANDING!

jokes.text: What the hell are you..? Oh, my couch! The arms, the seat-- The dream is over.
jokes.punchline: Couch breaks and Marge's dramatic mourning
jokes.explanation: Marge's over-dramatic reaction to furniture destruction

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer! What have you done?!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Our beautiful couch... it's ruined!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: This was a $300 couch!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: But honey, I was just trying to—

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Don't 'honey' me! I can't believe you would be so reckless and irresponsible!

jokes.text: We were sitting on the couch when we heard a creaking noise. We leapt off just in time to see it collapse. There, there. You're safe now, little sister.
jokes.punchline: Bart treating Lisa like she was in mortal danger from furniture
jokes.explanation: Bart's mock-heroic treatment of minor furniture mishap

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Lisa! Get away from the couch! It's gonna collapse!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Bart, it's just a couch.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Just a couch?! Lisa, these things are death traps! One wrong move and you're done for!

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: That's not how furniture works.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Oh sure, that's what they all say. Then WHAM! Crushed like a grape!

jokes.text: That's right. I shot J.R.
jokes.punchline: Homer claims responsibility for famous TV cliffhanger
jokes.explanation: Random pop culture reference without much setup or payoff

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I'm responsible for the 'Who Shot J.R.?' cliffhanger on Dallas. I was watching TV, fell asleep, and when I woke up, I thought someone had shot the TV.

jokes.text: Well, friend, you're going back where you came from-- the curb in front of Flanders's house.
jokes.punchline: Reveals couch was found garbage from neighbor
jokes.explanation: Reveals Homer's 'beloved' couch was actually Ned's discarded furniture

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Well, I guess we'll have to get a new couch.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, that couch cost us $3,000!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Don't worry, Marge. I found a perfectly good one in the garbage behind Flanders' house.

jokes.text: Lady, you just gave me... the idea of a lifetime. How do I thank you? Don't hurt me. Consider it done.
jokes.setup: Herb getting inspired by baby's crying
jokes.punchline: Mother's terrified response to his enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Mother's fear of homeless man's sudden enthusiasm is realistic

dialogue.speaker: Herb Powell
dialogue.line: That's it! That's the sound! A baby's cry! It's beautiful! I'm gonna build my fortune on it!

dialogue.speaker: Herb's Mother
dialogue.line: Oh my God, no! Please, anything but that!

jokes.text: Yeah, you going to be okay? Yeah. You know, my life just can't get any worse. That's right. There's no way my life could possibly get any worse.
jokes.setup: Homer declaring his life can't get worse
jokes.punchline: Repeated emphasis guarantees it will get worse
jokes.explanation: Classic sitcom setup for ironic reversal - well executed but predictable

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: My life can't get any worse.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: How many times have we seen that backfire?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: It can't get worse. It can't get worse. It can't get worse.

dialogue.speaker: Barney
dialogue.line: And there it goes.

jokes.text: D'oh!
jokes.setup: Homer summoned to Burns's office after saying life can't get worse
jokes.punchline: His signature frustrated exclamation
jokes.explanation: Perfect timing and setup payoff with Homer's catchphrase

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: D'oh!

jokes.text: Outstanding Achievement in the Field of... Excellence.
jokes.punchline: Completely meaningless circular award title
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly meaningless award name that became culturally quotable - Burns's improvisation at its finest

dialogue.speaker: Burns
dialogue.line: I hereby present this award to... the Employee of the People's Award for the Employees.

jokes.text: This is the biggest farce I ever saw. What about the Emmys? I stand corrected.
jokes.punchline: Reminded that Emmys are worse
jokes.explanation: Good setup-punchline structure mocking award show culture

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: This awards ceremony is a complete farce!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, at least it's not the Emmys.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: The Emmys? Those are way worse!

jokes.text: Webster's Dictionary defines excellence as 'the quality or condition of being excellent.'
jokes.punchline: Completely circular, meaningless definition
jokes.explanation: Perfect parody of meaningless award ceremony rhetoric with circular definition

jokes.text: Heavyweight championship. There's three of those. That couch was one of a kind.
jokes.setup: Frazier comparing losing championship to losing couch
jokes.punchline: Homer dismisses boxing title as common, couch as unique
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer logic - dismissing world championship as less valuable than furniture

dialogue.speaker: Joe Frazier
dialogue.line: Losing the championship was nothing compared to losing my couch.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Yeah, there's a lot of guys without a championship, but you're the only one without that couch.

jokes.text: All right! A peanut.
jokes.punchline: His excitement over finding single peanut
jokes.explanation: Shows Herb's desperation through simple excitement over trash food

dialogue.speaker: Herb Powell
dialogue.line: A peanut! A whole peanut!

jokes.text: Then how come you're still a bum? All right. A man needs two things-- an idea and money to get it off the ground.
jokes.setup: Herb claiming all man needs is idea
jokes.punchline: Forced to revise when confronted with his failure
jokes.explanation: Good deflation of motivational cliché when confronted with reality

dialogue.speaker: Herb Powell
dialogue.line: All a man needs is an idea. With an idea, a man can change the world!

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: So what happened to your idea, Herb?

dialogue.speaker: Herb Powell
dialogue.line: Well, I had to revise my philosophy.

jokes.text: Wow! The Spinemelter 2000.
jokes.punchline: Ridiculously aggressive name for massage chair
jokes.explanation: Perfect parody of extreme product naming for simple massage chair

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ooh, a massage chair! Let me just sit down and... *sits* Ahhhh, this is nice. What's this button do? *presses button* WHOA! WHOA! WHOA! This thing's trying to kill me!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Who names a massage chair the 'Spinal Realignment 3000: Vertebrae Destroyer'?!

jokes.text: There's an empty spot inside me. I tried to fill it with family, religion, community service, but those were dead ends. I think this chair is the answer.
jokes.setup: Homer explaining need for expensive chair
jokes.punchline: Dismisses meaningful life aspects as 'dead ends' compared to chair
jokes.explanation: Dark comedy gold - Homer's materialistic priorities over spiritual/family values

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Sure, I could spend time with my family, but they're just a dead end. This chair, though—this chair is going places.

jokes.text: Now excuse me while I kiss the sky.
jokes.setup: Homer giving up on chair purchase
jokes.punchline: Jimi Hendrix reference about getting high instead
jokes.explanation: Pop culture reference without much setup - relies on recognition rather than wit

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You know, I was going to buy that chair, but then I realized I could get high instead.

jokes.text: That's where you're wrong, friend. Today's our tithe day... and we've got ourselves a transient.
jokes.punchline: Ned excited to find homeless person to help for religious duty
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ned character moment - treating charity as convenient religious obligation

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: Sorry, wrong house.

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Wait! A homeless person! Thank the Lord! Finally, a chance to help those less fortunate and fulfill my Christian duty!

jokes.text: Dad, can I anoint the sores on his feet? I think it's Mom's turn, son. Oh, no fair.
jokes.punchline: Children treating grotesque charity as fun activity to take turns
jokes.explanation: Brilliant dark comedy - children eagerly competing to perform disgusting charity work

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Boys, we're going to help this less fortunate soul by taking turns carrying his belongings.

dialogue.speaker: Rod
dialogue.line: Yay! Can I go first, Dad?

dialogue.speaker: Tod
dialogue.line: No, me! Me first!

dialogue.speaker: Ned
dialogue.line: Now, now, Rod, Tod — there's plenty of homelessness to go around.

jokes.text: They're singing again. Lousy neighbors. Wish I was deaf.
jokes.punchline: Homer wishes for disability to avoid neighbors' happiness
jokes.explanation: Homer's extreme reaction to neighborly singing shows his character

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Dear God, I wish I was disabled so I wouldn't have to listen to the Flanders family singing.

jokes.text: I'm sorry, but I'm still mad at you. Every word you say makes me want to punch you. In my home, could you just kick me in the butt? I'll try, but I'm not making any promises.
jokes.setup: Herb expressing conflicted feelings about Homer
jokes.punchline: Homer's casual agreement to modified violence request
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer response - treating violence request as reasonable accommodation

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: I love you, but I also want to punch you in the face.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: How about the arm?

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: Okay.

jokes.text: I've been living in a cardboard box... sleeping on grates, eating out of Dumpsters. You? Can't complain.
jokes.punchline: Marge's understated response to dramatic contrast
jokes.explanation: Perfect understated response highlighting absurd contrast in life situations

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: I was so poor, I couldn't afford shoes. I had to wrap my feet in newspaper. And when it rained, the headlines would run and I'd get the news all over my socks.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Well, at least you were informed.

jokes.text: This is one of our many light switches. It functions in both the on and off mode. On, off. On, off. He knows how to work a light switch. Oh, yeah, right.
jokes.setup: Homer giving house tour to homeless brother
jokes.punchline: Overly explaining basic technology then being corrected
jokes.explanation: Homer's condescending assumption about homeless person's capabilities

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: And this is the kitchen. See, the cold box keeps food cold, and the hot box cooks it. It's called an oven.

dialogue.speaker: Grampa
dialogue.line: I thought you said it was a stove.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: No, no, Dad. A stove is different. This one has an oven.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Homer, they're basically the same thing.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Stay out of this, Marge!

jokes.text: I got the recipe from the Utility Grade Beef Council. They do good work.
jokes.punchline: Marge credits lowest-grade beef organization
jokes.explanation: Absurd industrial food organization with Marge's sincere endorsement

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: This is the best meal I've had in years, Marge. You're a tremendous cook.

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Oh, thank you, Herb. Well, I have to confess - I used the cheapest beef I could find.

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: Cheapest beef? But it tastes wonderful!

dialogue.speaker: Marge
dialogue.line: Yes, well, that's thanks to the D-Minus Meat Company. They really know how to organize their inventory.

jokes.text: Discarded pizza boxes... are an inexpensive source of cheese.
jokes.punchline: Herb's practical but disgusting survival tip
jokes.explanation: Grimly practical homeless advice delivered matter-of-factly

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: So Herb, what's your advice for surviving on the streets?

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: Well boy, I'll tell you - you gotta learn to eat out of dumpsters. Start with the Chinese restaurants, they throw out perfectly good food. Then work your way up to the Italian places.

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: And if you're really desperate, there's always the gas station sushi. Sure, it might kill ya, but at least you won't be hungry when it does.

jokes.text: Dear Lisa, last night... I used a rat for a pillow thanks to your pop
jokes.setup: Lisa asking why he didn't write
jokes.punchline: Herb's imagined letter with disturbing homeless detail
jokes.explanation: Dark escalation of homelessness details with specific disgusting imagery

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: Dear boy, I wanted to write, but I've been living under a bridge. The rats have taken my fingers. Well, not taken them—I sold them for a hot dog.

jokes.text: 1150 bones. That's all I got. Broke again, eh, Herb? Just like in real life. I guess you're just not much of a businessman.
jokes.punchline: Homer connecting game failure to real-life business disasters
jokes.explanation: Homer's cruel but accurate observation connecting game to reality

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Dad, you landed on Boardwalk. You owe me $2,000.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Two thousand dollars?! That's it, I'm bankrupt. This game is rigged!

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: It's just Monopoly, Dad.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Just Monopoly? Boy, this is exactly how my business ventures go. I make one bad decision, suddenly I'm homeless living in a box by the river.

jokes.text: We should get a machine gun. We could hunt game, spell out things... or ring in the new year.
jokes.setup: Family discussing how to spend money
jokes.punchline: Bart's increasingly absurd uses for machine gun
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation from hunting to celebration via machine gun

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: I could use a machine gun to enforce homework completion.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Or settle disputes over the TV remote.

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Heck, I could even use it to make sure everyone laughs at my jokes.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Son, that's the worst idea I've ever heard. I love it!

jokes.text: 'Paradise Regained, Martin Chuzzlewit... or Herman Melville's twin classics, Omoo and Typee.'
jokes.punchline: Obscure literary titles including Melville's lesser-known works
jokes.explanation: Good intellectual humor showing Lisa's literary knowledge and pretentious book marketing

dialogue.speaker: Lisa
dialogue.line: Oh, look at these obscure literary titles! 'Bartleby, the Scrivener,' 'Billy Budd, Sailor,' 'Pierre'... all those lesser-known Melville works!

jokes.text: It's drinking the water! Take it easy, Homer. This is an example... This is the greatest invention in the world. You'll make a million dollars. No, that invention is out already.
jokes.setup: Herb using drinking bird as example
jokes.punchline: Homer's immediate obsession disrupts presentation
jokes.explanation: Homer's inability to focus on presentation due to simple toy

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: See, this drinking bird here represents the American worker...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Ooh, a birdie!

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: No, Homer, it's a metaphor. If you wind it up, it keeps going and going...

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Wind it up? Gimme!

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: Homer, no! That's not how—

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Look at him go! Look at him go! Why won't he stop?!

jokes.text: Everything from 'change me' to 'turn off that damn Raffi record.'
jokes.punchline: Specific reference to annoying children's music
jokes.explanation: Relatable parent frustration with children's music translated through baby

dialogue.speaker: Herb Powell
dialogue.line: This baby translator can decipher any sound a baby makes. Crying, screaming, vomiting...

dialogue.speaker: Homer Simpson
dialogue.line: What's it saying now?

dialogue.speaker: Herb Powell
dialogue.line: It says your son wants you to stop playing that Barney song over and over.

jokes.text: Then give me... the drinking bird.
jokes.setup: Homer trying to negotiate with Herb
jokes.punchline: His obsession with simple toy over family relationship
jokes.explanation: Perfect Homer priorities - values toy over family reconciliation

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Mr. Powell, I know we haven't always seen eye to eye, but I'm willing to put the past behind us if you'll just give me that Buzz Lightyear action figure.

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: Homer, I'm your half-brother. Don't you think family is more important than some toy?

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: You're right, you're absolutely right. Family first... Now hand over the toy or I'll make you regret the day you were born.

jokes.text: I want to suck your nose. / Hooga-booga, hooga-booga. / I'm scared.
jokes.punchline: Absurd baby thoughts translated to English
jokes.explanation: Absurd baby thoughts work well for the device concept

dialogue.speaker: Baby Translator
dialogue.line: Maggie says: 'I wish I hadn't eaten that cupcake off the floor.'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: That's my girl!

jokes.text: This is the stupidest thing I've ever seen. I can't believe we blew 2,000 bucks on it... when right now... rollers could be kneading my buttocks. Could you stop thinking about your ass? I try, but I can't.
jokes.punchline: His honest admission about obsessing over massage chair
jokes.explanation: Homer's brutally honest self-assessment about his material obsessions

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: I don't need a baby translator. I know what Maggie wants.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: She wants me to stop thinking about that massage chair at the mall.

jokes.text: Lavish attention on me... and entertain me.
jokes.punchline: Baby demanding royal treatment
jokes.explanation: Funny insight into baby's imperious demands via translator

dialogue.speaker: Baby Translator
dialogue.line: The baby says she demands to be treated like royalty.

dialogue.speaker: Maggie
dialogue.line: *sucking on pacifier* Goo goo ga ga!

jokes.text: This is the stupidest thing I've ever seen. I can't believe we blew 2,000 bucks on it... when right now... rollers could be kneading my buttocks. Could you stop thinking about your ass? I try, but I can't.
jokes.punchline: Homer's honest admission about his buttocks obsession
jokes.explanation: Excellent character comedy showing Homer's self-awareness about his obsessions

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: This baby translator is a bunch of hooey. I don't need some dumb machine to tell me what my baby is thinking.

dialogue.speaker: Baby translator
dialogue.line: Baby says: 'I love you, Daddy.'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: See? Total garbage. He's probably saying something like 'Dad, you need to stop looking at your butt in the mirror.'

jokes.text: I have soiled myself. How embarrassing.
jokes.punchline: Baby expressing adult-like embarrassment about normal baby behavior
jokes.explanation: Adult emotional response to normal baby function creates humor

dialogue.speaker: Baby Translator Device
dialogue.line: What Maggie is saying is: 'Oh God, did everyone see that? I just spit up on myself. How embarrassing. I'm never going to live this down.'

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: She spit up? That's normal baby stuff!

dialogue.speaker: Baby Translator Device
dialogue.line: 'I know it's normal, but do they have to all stare at me like I'm some kind of animal? I have dignity, you know.'

jokes.text: I only want to eat candy. / Then that's all you'll get. / This leash demeans us both.
jokes.punchline: Baby's sophisticated complaint about leash dignity
jokes.explanation: Baby expressing adult philosophical concerns about personal dignity

dialogue.speaker: Baby
dialogue.line: This leash is an affront to my dignity as an infant.

dialogue.speaker: Parent
dialogue.line: It's for your safety, sweetie.

dialogue.speaker: Baby
dialogue.line: Safety? I'm already wearing a helmet that makes me look like a tiny astronaut. How much more protected can I possibly be?

jokes.text: Can I get armor-piercing, cyanide-tipped bullets? It's in the Constitution.
jokes.punchline: Herb's casual confirmation of extreme ammunition access
jokes.explanation: Dark satire of gun culture with constitutional justification

dialogue.speaker: Bart
dialogue.line: Wow, I'm a member of the NRA!

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: Sure, you can get all the ammunition you want.

jokes.text: I want what the dog's eating. / D'oh! / I'll get you... something nice.
jokes.setup: Herb offering Maggie anything she wants
jokes.punchline: Baby wants dog food, Homer's reaction, Herb's diplomatic response
jokes.explanation: Baby's honest desire for pet food with Homer's signature reaction

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: Now Maggie, you can have anything you want. Anything at all!

dialogue.speaker: Maggie
dialogue.line: *sucks pacifier*

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: She wants dog food!

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: Well, that's... that's very nutritious.

jokes.text: Homer, I bought you the damn chair.
jokes.punchline: Herb reveals he got Homer the material thing he wanted
jokes.explanation: Perfect payoff to Homer's material obsession during emotional family moment

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: Homer, I know we've had our differences, but I wanted to get you something special. Something you've always wanted.

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Really? What is it?

dialogue.speaker: Herb
dialogue.line: I got you a new TV!

dialogue.speaker: Homer
dialogue.line: Woo-hoo!
